coredata.dc:title: Tuning asymmetric electronic structure endows carbon dots with unexpected huge stokes shift for high contrast in vivo imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jingyu
Guo, Yifei
Geng, Xin
Wang, Junli
Li, Sen
Sun, Yuanqiang
Qu, Lingbo
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: In vivo imaging has become a key tool in biomedical studies because it provides a large amount of information about biological stimuli. Owing to the excellent biocompatibility and optical properties, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used for fluorescence bioimaging. Whereas strong background autofluorescence from tissues is one of the largest concerns with CDs-based in vivo imaging, the development of CDs with large Stokes shifts is becoming critical for reducing the background autofluorescence. Herein, a novel design strategy to increase the Stokes shift of CDs was developed. By breaking the symmetry of the electronic structure of the precursor, the Stokes shift was significantly increased from 75 nm in p-phenylenediamine-derived CDs to 161 nm in 2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine (NAP)-derived CDs. In addition, the asymmetric electronic structure also enhanced the intramolecular charge transfer effect, which endowed NAP-CDs with a sensitive response to micro-environment polarity. As cancer cells possess lower polarity than normal cells, NAP-CDs would be effectively applied for cancer imaging with remarkably reduced background fluorescence from cell, tissue, organ to in vivo levels. The asymmetric electronic structure strategy reported here provides an important theoretical basis for the rational design and effective synthesis of CDs with large Stokes shift for practical biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stokes shift
Carbon dots
In vivo
Polarity
Tumor imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell imaging of mitochondrially targeted CDs (Mito-520)
Design strategy to increase the Stokes shift of CDs
Optical properties and good biocompatibility of NAP-CDs
Extended Stokes shift endows NAP-CDs the ability to eliminate background fluorescence interference in cell imaging
Large Stokes shifts reduce background fluorescence during in vivo imaging
High-contrast fluorescence tumor imaging of NAP-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of NAP-CDs and the size distribution. (b) XPS spectrum of NAP-CDs. High-resolution XPS of NAP-CDs. (c) C 1 s spectra, (d) N 1 s spectra, and (e) O 1 s spectra. (f) FT-IR spectrum of NAP-CDs.

(a) The UV–vis absorption and FL emission spectra of NAP-CDs in PBS. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of NAP-CDs at various excitation wavelengths. (c) The in vitro photostability of NAP-CDs in PBS. (d) The fluorescence intensities of NAP-CDs in Britton-Robinson buffer solution at pH 5–12. (e) The fluorescence intensities of NAP-CDs to various analytes (1, NAP-CDs 2, Ala 3, Arg 4, Asp 5, Glu 6, His 7, Lys 8, Met 9, Phe 10, Pro 11, Thr 12, Trp 13, Val 14, Hcy 15, Cys 16, GSH 17, NaHSO3 18 NaHS) in PBS (pH = 7.4), the final concentration of each analyte is 200 μM. (f) The fluorescence intensities of NAP-CDs to various ions and ROS (1, NAP-CDs 2, Ag+ 3, Al3+ 4, Ca2+ 5, Cd2+ 6, Co2+ 7, Cr3+ 8, Cu2+ 9, Fe2+ 10, Hg2+ 11, Mg2+ 12, Ni2+ 13, Pb2+ 14, Zn2+ 15, ONOO– 16, ClO- 17, H2O2 18, O2 – 19, t-BuOOH) in PBS (pH = 7.4), the final concentration of each analyte is 200 μM. (g) The fluorescence intensities of NAP-CDs in various glycerol-water ratios. (h) The relative mean fluorescence levels of HeLa cells of NAP-CDs. (i) The relative mean fluorescence levels of HL 7702 cells of NAP-CDs.

Confocal fluorescence images of live HeLa cells cultured with NAP-CDs (a1 − a3), Mito-520 (b1–b3), and NAP-CDs/Mito-520 (c1–c3). The excitation wavelength is 488 nm. (a1, b1, c1) Green emission channel (500–545 nm), the laser intensity is Argon laser, 13 mW, the pinhole is 95.5 μm, the voltage is 30 V and the gain is 850 dB; (a2, b2, c2) Red emission channel (550–650 nm), the laser intensity is Argon laser, 13 mW, the pinhole is 95.5 μm, the voltage is 20 V and the gain is 750 dB; (a3, b3, c3) Overlay of green emission channel and red emission channels. Scale bars = 25 μm.

(a) In vivo imaging of supine nude mice at different emissions channels following subcutaneous injection of NAP-CDs (a1–a5) and Mito-520 on the left side and NAP-CDs in the right side (b1–b5). The images were collected at the excitation wavelength of 500 nm in the following ranges: 520–560 nm (a1–b1), 540–580 nm (a2–b2), 560–600 nm (a3–b3), 580–620 nm (a4–b4), 600–640 nm (a5–b5). (c) Semiquantitative fluorescent intensity of (b1-b6).

(a) Emission spectra of NAP-CDs in various DIOX/H2O ratios. (b) The linear relationship between the maximum emission fluorescence intensity and the solvent Δƒ. (c) Normalized emission spectra of NAP-CDs in various DIOX/H2O ratios. (d) Confocal fluorescence images of cancer cells (HeLa cells) and normal cells (HL 7702 cells) cultured with 30 μg·mL−1 NAP-CDs using the same parameters. Scale bars = 25 μm. (e) Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of NAP-CDs in tumor tissues and healthy tissues in the kidney, lung, spleen, liver, and heart. Scale bars = 25 μm. (f) Real-time ex vivo imaging of dissected organs and tumor incubated with NAP-CDs at different time points. (g) In vivo imaging of tumor-bearing mice following subcutaneous injection of NAP-CDs.
Design strategy for CDs with a large Stokes shift and tumor imaging using NAP-CDs.

head.ce:title: Tuning asymmetric electronic structure endows carbon dots with unexpected huge stokes shift for high contrast in vivo imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical Biology and Clinical Application at the First Affiliate Hospital

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China (Y. Sun, Z. Li).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In vivo imaging has become a key tool in biomedical studies because it provides a large amount of information about biological stimuli. Owing to the excellent biocompatibility and optical properties, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used for fluorescence bioimaging. Whereas strong background autofluorescence from tissues is one of the largest concerns with CDs-based in vivo imaging, the development of CDs with large Stokes shifts is becoming critical for reducing the background autofluorescence. Herein, a novel design strategy to increase the Stokes shift of CDs was developed. By breaking the symmetry of the electronic structure of the precursor, the Stokes shift was significantly increased from 75 nm in p-phenylenediamine-derived CDs to 161 nm in 2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine (NAP)-derived CDs. In addition, the asymmetric electronic structure also enhanced the intramolecular charge transfer effect, which endowed NAP-CDs with a sensitive response to micro-environment polarity. As cancer cells possess lower polarity than normal cells, NAP-CDs would be effectively applied for cancer imaging with remarkably reduced background fluorescence from cell, tissue, organ to in vivo levels. The asymmetric electronic structure strategy reported here provides an important theoretical basis for the rational design and effective synthesis of CDs with large Stokes shift for practical biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imaging is an imperative tool in biomedical studies which plays a crucial role in fundamental biological investigations and transforming clinical practice [1–3]. Among them, in vivo imaging has obtained increased emphasis on studying complex pathophysiological processes because it can reveal more complete information on biological stimuli [4]. Carbon dots (CDs), a type of molecular imaging probe, are well used for biological applications due to their excellent biocompatibility, optical stability, and low cytotoxicity [5–14]. However, the strong background autofluorescence from tissues is the main obstacle for CDs-based in vivo imaging [15,16]. The background autofluorescence originates from a large number of endogenous biological fluorophores in tissues, which usually possess small Stokes shifts. For example, flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD), the most common fluorophore responsible for endogenous fluorescence from biological samples, exhibits absorption and emission at 450 nm and 515 nm, and the Stokes shift is only 65 nm [17–19]. Therefore, biological background fluorescence could be effectively reduced by using CDs with a large Stokes shift, which would be of great benefit for in vivo imaging.

To date, only a small number of CDs with a large Stokes shift has been reported [20–25], and almost all of them have emissions in the green to yellow region. Studies have found that the photodamage of green to yellow-emission CDs is more severe than that of red-emission CDs, and the tissue permeability is much lower than that of red-emission CDs [26–31]. What’s more, almost all the reported CDs with large Stokes shift are from trial-and-error, no rational design strategies exist to fine-tune the Stokes shift of CDs. The absence of such a design rationale has become a big obstruct to conducting in-depth biological and medical research. Ideal CDs for in vivo imaging should satisfy the following three criteria: (1) a large Stokes shift to eliminate background autofluorescence. (2) emission in the red regions to increase tissue permeability. (3) good biocompatibility. Thus, there is an urgent need for novel strategies to synthesize highly efficient red-emissive CDs with a large Stokes shift.

Herein, a novel design strategy for preparing red-emissive CDs with a large Stokes shift is reported. As seen in Scheme 1 , by breaking the symmetry of the electronic structures to increase the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) ability of the precursors, the Stokes shift of CDs gradually increases from 75 nm (p-phenylenediamine–derived CDs, p-CDs), 100 nm (N-phenyl-1,2-phenylenediamine–derived CDs, PPh-CDs) to 161 nm (2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine–derived CDs, NAP-CDs). Meanwhile, the emission of CDs remains in the red regions. The advantages of a large Stokes shift and red emission were demonstrated by using NAP-CDs. The in vivo experiments showed that the large Stokes shift of NAP-CDs could effectively reduce the interference caused by the background fluorescence of the organism. Due to the increased ICT effect, NAP-CDs presented high sensitivity to the micro-environment polarity. As cancer cells have lower polarity than normal cells [32], these properties of NAP-CDs enabled the successful application of cancer imaging from cell, tissue, organ, to in vivo level.

Briefly, 0.05 g of 2-Nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine was added into 5 mL of ethanol solution and lysed by sonication. The resulting solution was transferred to autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Silica gel column chromatography was performed to purify the crude products and the eluent was composed of ethyl acetate and petroleum ether (V: V = 5:4). Ultimately, the obtained NAP-CDs were dispersed in DMSO and the final concentration was 10 mg⋅mL−1.

MTT experiment was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of NAP-CDs. HeLa cells and HL 7702 cells were used as a demonstration, which were cultured using dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) combined with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin as cell culture medium. After cells were adherent to the 96-well plates in a 24 h incubation, a gradient of NAP-CDs (0 – 50 μg·mL−1) diluted in DMEM were performed to replace the cell culture medium and incubated for another 24 h. A volume of 90 μL of DMEM and 10 μL of MTT (5 mg⋅mL−1) was added and incubated. After 4 h, the supernatant solution was substituted with 100 μL of DMSO to dissolve MTT. The cell cytotoxicity was calculated by using the absorbance of the solution at 570 nm.

HeLa cells were applied as a demonstration to assess the ability of NAP-CDs for cell imaging. HeLa cells were cultured with cell culture medium in 1 mL Petri dishes until tightly adherent. Washing cells three times to remove the nonadherent cells via 10 mM PBS (pH = 7.4). Then the cells were incubated in DMEM combined with 30 μg·mL−1 NAP-CDs for 5 min. SP-8X Leica laser scanning confocal microscope (λex = 488 nm, λem = 550 – 650 nm) was used to image.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging of mitochondrially targeted CDs (Mito-520)
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were cultured with cell culture medium in 1 mL Petri dishes until tightly adherent. Then the cells were incubated in DMEM combined with 80 μg·mL−1 Mito-520 for 4 h. Washing cells three times to remove the nonadherent cells via 10 mM PBS (pH = 7.4). SP-8X Leica laser scanning confocal microscope (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500 – 545 nm) was used to image.

Hela tumor-bearing female BALB/c-nu mice (5 weeks old) were provided by Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co. Ltd. The lower background fluorescence performance of NAP-CDs compared with Mito-520 in nude mice is evaluated with 540 nm, 560 nm, 580 nm, 600 nm, and 620 nm emission under 500 nm excitation. Two 5-week-old female nude mice were separated into two groups (n = 1): one group was subcutaneously injected with NAP-CDs (100 μg·mL−1), the other group was subcutaneously injected with NAP-CDs and Mito-520 (200 μg·mL−1), respectively. Then, the in vivo imaging was performed via an IVIS Lumina system (PerkinElmer, WA, USA). The results were analyzed by the Living Image® 4.7 software with uniform settings to guarantee data consistency.

NAP-CDs' tumor imaging performance in nude mice is evaluated with 600 nm emission under 500 nm excitation. 5-week-old female tumor-bearing mice were used for in vivo imaging. The same dose (100 μL 100 μg·mL−1) of NAP-CDs was injected into the tumor site and symmetrically subcutaneously in mice. Then, the in vivo imaging by using NAP-CDs was performed via an IVIS Lumina system (PerkinElmer, WA, USA). The results were analyzed by the Living Image® 4.7 software with uniform settings to guarantee data consistency. No animal behaved any signs of acute toxicological responses during the experiments.

The organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumor were isolated from the mice. After being washed three times with PBS, the organs and tumor were soaked with NAP-CDs (100 μg·mL−1) for 0, 5, 10, and 15 min. Then the fluorescence images were performed via an IVIS Lumina system under 500 nm excitation and 600 nm emission (PerkinElmer, WA, USA).

The organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumor were soaked with NAP-CDs for 15 min and frozen at optimal temperature for slices cut. Frozen sections were cut for histological analysis. SP-8X Leica laser scanning confocal microscope was used to image without soaking NAP-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design strategy to increase the Stokes shift of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: p-Phenylenediamine has been widely employed as an effective precursor for preparing CDs since it was first reported by Lin’s group in 2015 [33]. However, the Stokes shift of the p-phenylenediamine–derived CDs is only 75 nm, which does not meet the requirement for in vivo imaging. Recently, we reported polarity-sensitive CDs with a Stokes shift of 100 nm, obtained by simply changing the precursor from p-phenylenediamine to N-phenyl-1,2-phenylenediamine [34]. A small change in the N-substitution of the p-phenylenediamine precursor conferred a larger Stokes shift to the CDs, which resulted from the increased electron-donating ability of the N atom and led to an increase in the ICT effect. These results inspired us to develop novel CDs with a larger Stokes shift to meet the demands of in vivo imaging. Therefore, we conjectured that the Stokes shifts of CDs could be further increased by introducing electron-withdrawing or electron-donating groups that would break the symmetrical electronic structures of the precursor [35]. To corroborate this conjecture, we synthesized NAP-CDs using 2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine as the precursor. The nitro-group is a well-known electron-withdrawing group [36,37], thus the symmetric electronic structure of the p-phenylenediamine was broken by the asymmetric structure and strong electron-donating ability of the amino and N-phenyl groups, as well as the strong electron-withdrawing nitro group. As seen in Scheme 1, the Stokes shift of the NAP-CDs enjoyed noticeably increased from 75 to 161 nm. The results indicated that breaking the symmetric electronic structures of the precursor could effectively increase the Stokes shift of CDs.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images show the spherical structure of NAP-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1
a, the prepared NAP-CDs have small particle sizes and Gaussian size distribution with an average diameter of approximately 2.8 nm, indicating that NAP-CDs were successfully synthesized. The chemical composition of NAP-CDs and the valence states of C, N, and O were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS results revealed that the NAP-CDs were composed of C, N, and O (Fig. 1 b). The binding states of different elements are presented in Fig. 1 c–1e. High-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra revealed that there are two binding states, namely C = C/C–C (284.5 eV) and C–N (285.1 eV). The N 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra elucidated the features of the nitro-groups, as well as three binding states: graphitic (398.5 eV), C–N = C (399.1 eV), and pyridinic N (400.1 eV). NAP-CDs also had a binding state of N 1 s at 400.9 eV, which is indicative of = N–O. The O 1 s XPS spectra featured three peaks centered at 531.6 eV, 532.4 eV, and 533.2 eV, typical to C = O, C–O–C, and = N–O. Together, the O 1 s and N 1 s XPS spectra revealed the presence of the nitro group. The NAP-CDs were further scrutinized by Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 f, the peaks at 3400 and 3200 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H, the absorption band at 1330 cm−1 belong to the bending vibrations of N–H which is consistent with the central peak of p-CDs and PPh-CDs ( Fig. S1 ). The symmetric peaks at 1648 and 1510 cm−1 attributable to the nitro group were consistent with the XPS analysis. With its strong electron-withdrawing effect, the nitro group is responsible for breaking the symmetry of the electronic structure of p-phenylenediamine and thereby significantly increasing the Stokes shift of NAP-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and good biocompatibility of NAP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra were utilized to study the optical properties of NAP-CDs. It can be depicted from Fig. 2
a, the maximum absorption of the NAP-CDs in PBS appears at 449 nm and the optimal emission wavelength is 610 nm. The NAP-CDs possessed a huge Stokes shift of 161 nm in PBS which is obviously enhanced compared with p-CDs (75 nm) and PPh-CDs (100 nm) caused by breaking the symmetric electronic structures of the precursor. At the same time, the extended Stokes shift would effectively reduce the background fluorescence and therefore be advantageous for in vivo imaging. The NAP-CDs behaved red emission with the optimal emission at 610 nm in PBS, indicating the possible deep-tissue permeability of NAP-CDs. Meanwhile, the emission behavior of NAP-CDs is independent of excitation with a peak at 610 nm (Fig. 2 b), which is different from most of the reported CDs, indicating that the surface state and size distribution of the NAP-CDs are stable and uniform [8,38]. The in vitro photostability of the NAP-CDs was also studied. After 60 min of continuous irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of NAP-CDs was almost unchanged (Fig. 2 c), indicating the brilliant photostability of NAP-CDs in PBS buffer. It is noteworthy that even if NAP-CDs were stored at room temperature and protected from light for 90 days, there was almost no shift of the maximum absorption peak and the maximum emission peak, indicating the physical stability of NAP-CDs is fascinating. Simultaneously, the possible impact of pH values, biological substances, and viscosity was also explored. As seen in Fig. 2 d, the fluorescence intensity of the NAP-CDs is similar at different pH values. The fluorescence intensity remains unchanged when biological substances including amino acids, reactive oxygen species, reactive sulfur species, and cations were added (Fig. 2 e and f). As shown in Fig. 2 g, the effect of viscosity on NAP-CDs is so sighted that it is almost negligible. All these results demonstrated that NAP-CDs owned robust optical stability in complex biological environments.

Large Stokes shift and red-emission endow NAP-CDs the possibility of high-contrast in vivo imaging. The cytotoxicity of NAP-CDs is another criterion for in vivo imaging, we evaluate it on HeLa cells (cancer cells) and HL 7702 cells (epithelial cells). As can be seen from Fig. 2 h and i, marked changes in the survival rate of cancer cells and epithelial cells are not observed after culturing with NAP-CDs. Besides, the cell viability of cancer cells and epithelial cells viabilities are both over 95% after 24 h incubation with 50 μg⋅mL−1 NAP-CDs, indicating the excellent biocompatibility of NAP-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extended Stokes shift endows NAP-CDs the ability to eliminate background fluorescence interference in cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The permeability of NAP-CDs in HeLa cells was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2 there are apparent fluorescence signals observable in 50 s. The existing data indicated the fabulous permeability of NAP-CDs. In addition, the effect of concentration on imaging HeLa cells was also investigated. As the concentration of NAP-CDs increased up to 30 μg·mL−1, the fluorescence intensity increased and then slightly decreased ( Fig. S3 ). Therefore, 30 μg·mL−1 was chosen as the optimal concentration. Simultaneously, the photostability of NAP-CDs was also evaluated. The results are shown in Fig. S4 , and the fluorescence intensity mainly remained constant over time, showing the robust stability of NAP-CDs.

Owing to the large Stokes shift of NAP-CDs, we tested their potential for cell imaging to eliminate background fluorescence interference. Mito-520 was reported by our group in 2019 [39]. Because Mito-520 exhibits excitation and emission at 488 nm and 520 nm, which is consistent with the common endogenous fluorophore FAD, Mito-520 was used to simulate the background fluorescence. 80 μg·mL−1 Mito-520 were incubated with live HeLa cells for 4 h and then 30 μg·mL−1 NAP-CDs for 5 min, followed by being imaged with an SP-8X Leica laser scanning confocal microscope at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm. The emission was selected from channels 500–545 nm and 550–650 nm. By virtue of the larger Stokes shift, the emission of NAP-CDs was mainly concentrated in the red channel and there was almost no fluorescence in the green channel (Fig. 3
a1–3a3), demonstrating that larger Stokes shift could effectively eliminate interference induced by the endogenous fluorophore. In contrast, the situation was the opposite of Mito-520, with no fluorescence in the red channel and the emission of Mito-520 was mainly concentrated in the green channel (Fig. 3 b1–3b3). Subsequently, NAP-CDs and Mito-520 were incubated together with live HeLa cells and confocal imaging was performed. The simultaneous imaging of the cells in different channels under the same excitation was observed in Fig. 3 c1–3c3. Clearly, the characteristic of the large Stokes shift of NAP-CDs endowed their potential for in vivo imaging with eliminating background autofluorescence interference. Meanwhile, the results elucidated that NAP-CDs could be multicolor imaging candidates for studying intracellular interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Large Stokes shifts reduce background fluorescence during in vivo imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
In vivo imaging is critical for studying complex pathophysiological processes as it can provide more complete information on biological stimuli than other imaging methods. However, the strong background autofluorescence from tissues is one of the main drawbacks of in vivo imaging using CDs. The cell experiment proved that the large Stokes shift of CDs could effectively eliminate the interference caused by the background auto-fluorescence. Herein, live-animal imaging was performed to investigate the advantages of the large Stokes shift of NAP-CDs. NAP-CDs (100 μg·mL−1 0.1 mL) were subcutaneously injected into mice and the mice were irradiated at 500 nm. The emission spectra were measured at 540 nm, 560 nm, 580 nm, 600 nm, and 620 nm. Five images of the mice are shown as examples in Fig. 4
a1–4a5. The fluorescence intensity of background autofluorescence was much weaker than that of NAP-CDs at all five emission wavelengths, confirming that a large Stokes shift can effectively reduce the interference caused by the background autofluorescence of the organism. In addition, Mito-520 was selected for comparison, which owned good biocompatibility and excellent properties but suffered from a small Stokes shift. NAP-CDs (100 μg·mL−1 0.1 mL) and Mito-520 (200 μg·mL−1 0.1 mL) were subcutaneously injected into mice and the mice were irradiated at 500 nm. The emission spectra were measured at 540 nm, 560 nm, 580 nm, 600 nm, and 620 nm. As shown in Fig. 4 b1, there is basically no difference between the fluorescence of Mito-520 and that of NAP-CDs. Whereas, with the redshift of emission wavelength, the contrast between the fluorescence of NAP-CDs and that of Mito-520 increases obviously (Fig. 4 b2–4b3), which shows that the larger Stokes shift the more favorable it is to eliminate the interference of background fluorescence. The interference effect of background auto-fluorescence of these two probes in vivo imaging was analyzed by semi-quantitative analysis, it is found that there is basically no difference between Mito-520 and background auto-fluorescence. Still, there is a big difference between NAP-CDs and background auto-fluorescence (Fig. 4 c), which indicates that the large Stokes shift can effectively reduce the interference of background auto-fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-contrast fluorescence tumor imaging of NAP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Polarity plays an important role in chemistry and biology, and abnormal changes in polarity are closely related to diseases [40]. Furthermore, research revealed that cancer cells possess lower polarity than normal cells. Hence, monitoring the cell polarity is a potential method for tumor imaging. By breaking the symmetry of the electronic structures of the precursor, the Stokes shift of NAP-CDs was significantly increased with a concomitant increase in the ICT effect. More interestingly, the increased ICT effect endowed NAP-CDs with excellent responsiveness to the micro-environment polarity. The photophysical response of NAP-CDs toward micro-environment polarity was studied in various solvents. As diagrammed in Fig. S5
a, the fluorescence intensity increases with an increase in the solvent polarity. At the same time, the emission spectra of NAP-CDs are shifted to a longer wavelength from dioxane (559 nm) to water (610 nm), denoting a 51 nm bathochromic shift ( Fig. S5
b), which indicated the strong positive solvatochromism of NAP-CDs. In addition, NAP-CDs had a linear relationship between their maximum emission wavelength and the solvent polarity parameter (ET30) ( Fig. S5
c). Various 1,4-dioxane/H2O ratios were applied to test the polarity sensitivity of NAP-CDs. From 1,4-dioxane to H2O, there was a shift in the emissions spectra from 559 nm to 610 nm (Fig. 5
c), with obvious decay in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5 a). Notably, only 10% H2O was sufficient to reduce the fluorescence intensity of NAP-CDs by more than five times (Fig. 5 a). Moreover, there was an excellent linear relationship between the maximum fluorescence intensity and the well-known Lippert Mataga polarity parameter (Δƒ) (R2 = 0.9607) (Fig. 5 b). These data also suggested that NAP-CDs were extremely sensitive to the slight changes in the micro-environment polarity owing to the increased ICT effect.

Owing to their strong response to small changes in polarity, the synthesized NAP-CDs were used for tumor imaging at the cell level. HeLa cells (cancer cell line) and HL 7702 cells (normal cell line) were used for the experiments. As expected, the fluorescence in the cancer cell line is much stronger than that in the normal cell line (Fig. 5 d). After evaluating the performance of NAP-CDs for tumor imaging at the cell level, we studied the feasibility of tumor imaging at the tissue level. The results are shown in Fig. 5 e, there is essentially no fluorescence in normal tissue relative to the tumor tissue. These data indicated that the tumor-discriminating performance of NAP-CDs was extremely good. In addition, the performance of NAP-CDs for tumor imaging in organs was tested. Four representative organ images are shown in Fig. 5 f. The results agreed with the tissue imaging data, with a clearly enhanced fluorescence signal in the tumor compared with the normal organs. As the incubation time increased, the fluorescence intensity of the normal organ remained essentially unchanged. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the tumor organ increased continuously over the first 10 min and then remained largely unchanged. Finally, a preliminary assessment of NAP-CDs for tumor imaging in vivo was performed. As seen in Fig. 5 g, the fluorescence signal in the tumor can be clearly observed, along with the fluorescence signal in the healthy tissue that is much weaker than that in the tumor. The fluorescence signal of NAP-CDs was stronger than the background autofluorescence (Fig. 4), and the fluorescence signal of the tumor location was visibly stronger than that of the healthy tissue, making the background fluorescence of the organism almost negligible compared with the fluorescence signal from the tumor.

Overall, the data indicate that NAP-CDs could be candidates for tumor imaging from cell, tissue, organ to in vivo levels. The fluorescence intensity in the tumor tissue was obviously stronger than that in normal tissue, demonstrating that NAP-CDs could be effectively used for cancer diagnosis. Thus, the present results elucidate that NAP-CDs are candidate molecules for use in clinical diagnosis and image-guided surgery.

A novel design strategy to prepare red-emissive CDs with a large Stokes shift was reported. Increasing the ICT ability of the precursors by introducing a substituent to break the symmetrical electronic structure of the precursor enabled NAP-CDs with good optical properties and a notably extended Stokes shift. This large Stokes shift of NAP-CDs made them suitable for in vivo imaging with reduced background autofluorescence and gave the possibility for multicolor imaging. As the micro-environment polarity increased, the fluorescence intensity of NAP-CDs efficiently decreased due to the increased ICT effect. The prepared NAP-CDs were successfully used for cancer imaging from cell, tissue, organ to in vivo level. In addition, the effect of the carbon precursor’s electronic structure on the Stokes shift of the obtained CDs was investigated. This strategy provides a rational design for CDs with a large Stokes shift and should significantly enhance the development of other nanomaterial-based imaging probes.

Jingyu Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yifei Guo: Investigation, Software. Xin Geng: Investigation, Software. Junli Wang: Software. Sen Li: Software. Yuanqiang Sun: Conceptualization, Project administration, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Lingbo Qu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhaohui Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974125), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (22TRTSTHN002), 111 Project of Henan Province (CXJD2021001), Zhongyuan Scholars Program (202101510005), and Key Project of Scientific and Technological in Henan Province (212102311059).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.136928.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
